Norge.general: Tipping is not expected but always appreciated — a small gesture of 5–10% signals genuine satisfaction.
Norge.restaurants: Tipping is not expected, but 5–10% for good service is a warm gesture.
Norge.taxis: Round up to the nearest 10 NOK, or add 10% for excellent service.
Norge.services: 5–10% for hairdressers and spa treatments is appreciated but not obligatory.

Sverige.general: Tipping is not obligatory, but a small tip for good service is always well received.
Sverige.restaurants: Not customary, but 5–10% is welcome for good service.
Sverige.taxis: Round up or add 10% for helpful drivers.
Sverige.services: 5–10% for hairdressers and spa services is appreciated.

Danmark.general: Tipping is not required; service is included in prices, but small gestures are warmly received.
Danmark.restaurants: Not expected, but 5–10% is a warm gesture for good service.
Danmark.taxis: Round up to the nearest 10 DKK.

Finland.general: Tipping is uncommon and not expected, but a small amount for excellent service is appreciated.
Finland.restaurants: Not expected, but 5–10% for excellent service is appreciated.
Finland.services: 5–10% for hairdressers and massage is a kind gesture.

Island.general: Tipping is not part of local culture; service charges are built into most bills.
Island.restaurants: Tipping is not traditional; service charges are often built into the bill.
Island.services: Service charges are usually included; extra tips are not expected.

Storbritannia.general: Tipping is expected for good service, especially in restaurants and taxis — 10–15% is the norm.
Storbritannia.restaurants: 10–12.5% service charge is often added; if not, 10–15% is customary.
Storbritannia.services: 10–15% for hairdressers and beauty services is standard.

Irland.general: Tipping for good service is common practice, typically 10–15% in restaurants and for taxis.
Irland.restaurants: 10–15% is common if service charge is not already included.
Irland.taxis: Round up or add €1–2 for a helpful driver.
Irland.services: 10% for hairdressers and beauty treatments is appreciated.

Frankrike.general: Service is legally included in all bills, but an extra tip for great service is a warm gesture.
Frankrike.restaurants: Service is legally included (service compris), but an extra 5–10% for great service is welcomed.
Frankrike.taxis: Round up to the nearest euro.
Frankrike.services: 5–10% for hairdressers and spa treatments is customary.

Italia.general: A cover charge (coperto) is standard; tipping on top is appreciated but not obligatory.
Italia.restaurants: A coperto (cover charge) is common; an extra 5–10% tip is appreciated but not required.
Italia.taxis: Round up to the nearest euro.
Italia.services: 5–10% for hairdressers and beauty services is appreciated.

Spania.general: Service is usually included; small extra tips are welcome but not expected.
Spania.restaurants: Service charge is usually included; leaving small change or 5–10% extra is appreciated.
Spania.taxis: Round up to the nearest euro.
Spania.services: 5–10% for hairdressers and personal care is customary.

Portugal.general: Tipping is not mandatory but 5–10% for good service is appreciated.
Portugal.restaurants: Not mandatory, but 5–10% for good service is appreciated.
Portugal.taxis: Round up to the nearest euro.
Portugal.services: 5–10% for hairdressers and personal services is a kind gesture.

Tyskland.general: Tipping is expected — round up or state the total including your tip when paying.
Tyskland.restaurants: State the rounded total when paying — 5–10% above the bill is the norm.
Tyskland.taxis: Round up 10% and state the total amount.
Tyskland.services: 5–10% for hairdressers and personal care is expected.

Nederland.general: Rounding up or adding 5–10% for good service is customary and appreciated.
Nederland.restaurants: Round up or add 5–10% for good service.
Nederland.taxis: Round up to the nearest euro.
Nederland.services: 5–10% for hairdressers and personal care is appreciated.

Belgia.general: Service is usually included; a small extra tip for good service is a friendly gesture.
Belgia.restaurants: Service charge is usually included; an extra 5–10% is a welcome gesture.
Belgia.taxis: Round up to the nearest euro.
Belgia.services: 5–10% for hairdressers and personal services is appreciated.

Sveits.general: Service is typically included; an extra 5–10% for excellent service is appreciated.
Sveits.restaurants: Service is usually included; an extra 5–10% for excellent service is appreciated.
Sveits.taxis: Round up 10%; drivers often appreciate a small gratuity.
Sveits.services: 5–10% for hairdressers and spa treatments is customary.

Østerrike.general: Tipping is customary — state the rounded total when paying rather than leaving cash on the table.
Østerrike.restaurants: Tell the waiter the rounded total when paying; 5–10% is customary.
Østerrike.taxis: Round up 10% and state the total.
Østerrike.services: 10% for hairdressers and personal services is standard.

Hellas.general: Tipping is common, especially in tourist areas — 5–10% for good service is appreciated.
Hellas.restaurants: 5–10% is appreciated; service charge may or may not be included.
Hellas.taxis: Round up to the nearest euro.
Hellas.services: 5–10% for hairdressers and personal care is customary.

Tyrkia.general: Tipping is widely expected; 10–15% in restaurants and rounding up for taxis is the norm.
Tyrkia.restaurants: 10–15% is expected if a service charge is not included in the bill.
Tyrkia.taxis: Round up to the nearest lira.
Tyrkia.shops: No tipping expected, though tour guides appreciate tips.
Tyrkia.services: 10% for hairdressers and personal services is standard.

Polen.general: Tipping is common and appreciated — 10% is the standard for good service.
Polen.restaurants: 10% is the norm for good service in restaurants.
Polen.taxis: Round up to the nearest zloty or add 10%.
Polen.services: 10% for hairdressers and personal care is appreciated.

Tsjekkia.general: Tipping is customary; state the total you want to pay, including a 10–15% gratuity.
Tsjekkia.restaurants: 10–15% is appreciated; round up or state the total amount.
Tsjekkia.taxis: Round up 10% and state the total.
Tsjekkia.services: 10% for hairdressers and personal services is expected.

Ungarn.general: Tipping is standard practice; 10% is expected in most service situations.
Ungarn.restaurants: 10% is customary; some restaurants automatically add a service charge.
Ungarn.taxis: Round up or add 10% for helpful service.
Ungarn.services: 10% for hairdressers is standard; spa services often include gratuity.

Kroatia.general: Tipping is appreciated and increasingly common, especially in tourist areas.
Kroatia.restaurants: 10% for good service is appreciated; service charge is not always included.
Kroatia.taxis: Round up to the nearest euro.
Kroatia.services: 10% for hairdressers and personal services is customary.

Romania.general: Tipping is common and expected — 10% in cash is the norm for good service.
Romania.restaurants: 10% is the norm; leave cash on the table or state the amount when paying.
Romania.taxis: Round up or add 10% for good service.
Romania.services: 10% for hairdressers and personal care is appreciated.

Bulgaria.general: Tipping is common and appreciated; 10% is the standard for good service.
Bulgaria.restaurants: 10% is customary and appreciated for good service.
Bulgaria.taxis: Round up to the nearest lev.
Bulgaria.services: 10% for hairdressers and personal services is standard.

Russland.general: Tipping in cash is customary in restaurants and for personal services — 10% is the norm.
Russland.restaurants: 10% is customary in sit-down restaurants, usually left in cash.
Russland.taxis: Agree on a price beforehand; small tips are appreciated.
Russland.services: 10% for hairdressers and personal services is standard.

USA.general: Tipping is an integral part of the service economy — 15–20% is the expected minimum.
USA.restaurants: 15–20% is the standard expected tip for sit-down restaurants.
USA.taxis: 15–20% tip is expected; rideshare apps also prompt for tips.
USA.shops: No tipping in shops, but tip jars are common at cafés.
USA.services: 15–20% for hairdressers, massage, and personal services is standard.

Canada.general: Tipping culture closely mirrors the US — 15–20% is expected for most services.
Canada.restaurants: 15–20% is the standard tip for sit-down restaurants, similar to the US.
Canada.shops: No tipping in shops, but tip jars are common at cafés.
Canada.services: 15–20% for hairdressers and personal services is the norm.

Mexico.general: Tipping is common and important for service workers who rely on gratuities.
Mexico.restaurants: 10–15% tip is customary; service charge is sometimes included.
Mexico.taxis: Round up or add 10–15% for good service.
Mexico.services: 10–15% for hairdressers and personal services is appreciated.

Brasil.general: A 10% service charge is usually included; tipping beyond that is a generous gesture.
Brasil.restaurants: 10% service charge (taxa de serviço) is usually included; extra tips are welcome.
Brasil.taxis: Round up to the nearest real.
Brasil.services: 10% for hairdressers and personal services is customary.

Argentina.general: Tipping is common and important; service workers rely on tips to supplement low wages.
Argentina.restaurants: 10% is customary; service staff rely on tips as wages are low.
Argentina.taxis: Round up; car services (remises) do not expect tips.
Argentina.services: 10% for hairdressers and personal services is standard.

Chile.general: A 10% propina is standard and sometimes added automatically.
Chile.restaurants: 10% propina is customary and sometimes automatically added.
Chile.taxis: Round up to the nearest peso.
Chile.services: 10% for hairdressers and personal services is appreciated.

Colombia.general: A 10% propina is the norm; often added automatically in upscale restaurants.
Colombia.restaurants: 10% propina is standard; some restaurants include it automatically.
Colombia.taxis: Round up or add small change.
Colombia.services: 10% for hairdressers and personal care is customary.

Peru.general: Tipping is appreciated, especially in tourist restaurants and for guides.
Peru.restaurants: 10% is expected in mid-range and upscale restaurants.
Peru.taxis: Negotiate the fare beforehand; tips are not expected.
Peru.services: 10% for hairdressers and personal care is appreciated.

Ecuador.general: Tipping is appreciated; 10% is customary when a service charge is not included.
Ecuador.restaurants: 10% service charge is sometimes included; if not, 10% tip is appreciated.
Ecuador.taxis: Round up to the nearest dollar.

Uruguay.general: A 10% propina is standard practice in most service situations.
Uruguay.restaurants: 10% propina is standard in most restaurants.
Uruguay.taxis: Round up to the nearest peso.
Uruguay.services: 10% for hairdressers and personal services is appreciated.

Japan.general: Tipping is not practiced and can be considered impolite — excellent service is simply expected.
Japan.restaurants: Tipping is not customary and can be considered rude — excellent service is simply the standard.
Japan.taxis: No tipping; hand the exact fare or accept your change.
Japan.services: Do not tip for any service; gratitude is expressed through politeness, not money.

Kina.general: Tipping is not traditional but is becoming more accepted in tourist areas and luxury venues.
Kina.restaurants: Tipping is not traditional, but is becoming accepted in tourist areas and upscale restaurants.
Kina.taxis: No tipping expected; use the meter.
Kina.services: 5–10% at upscale hotels is welcomed, but not expected in general.

Sør-Korea.general: Tipping is not customary and can feel awkward; excellent service is its own reward.
Sør-Korea.restaurants: Tipping is not customary and can feel awkward; service charges are included at upscale venues.
Sør-Korea.taxis: No tipping; use the metered fare.
Sør-Korea.services: No tipping in traditional services; some international-style spas may accept tips.

Thailand.general: Tipping is appreciated but not obligatory; small tips are a kind gesture for good service.
Thailand.restaurants: 20–50 THB per person for good service is appreciated; some restaurants add a service charge.
Thailand.taxis: Round up to the nearest 5 THB; for tuk-tuks, negotiate the fare.
Thailand.services: 50–100 THB for massage and beauty services is standard and appreciated.

Vietnam.general: Tipping is not required but appreciated, especially in tourist areas and for personal services.
Vietnam.restaurants: Not required, but 10% is appreciated in tourist restaurants.
Vietnam.taxis: Round up; negotiate fares for motorbike taxis.
Vietnam.services: 20,000–50,000 VND for massage and spa services is a kind gesture.

Indonesia.general: Tipping is not obligatory but small tips for good service are welcomed.
Indonesia.restaurants: 5–10% if no service charge is included; leave cash on the table.
Indonesia.taxis: Round up; negotiate rates with ride-hailing apps.
Indonesia.services: 5–10% for massage and personal services is appreciated.

Malaysia.general: Service charges are often included; extra tipping is a welcome but not required gesture.
Malaysia.restaurants: Service charge and government tax are often included; extra tipping is welcome but not required.
Malaysia.taxis: No tipping expected; use the meter or agree on a fare.
Malaysia.services: 5–10% for personal services is a kind gesture.

Filippinene.general: Tipping is appreciated for good service and is becoming more common in urban areas.
Filippinene.restaurants: 10% is appreciated if a service charge is not already included.
Filippinene.taxis: Round up to the nearest peso.
Filippinene.services: 10% for hairdressers and personal care is standard.

Singapore.general: Service charge and GST are almost always included — no additional tipping is needed.
Singapore.restaurants: 10% service charge and 9% GST are almost always included; no additional tip needed.
Singapore.taxis: No tipping expected; meters are used.
Singapore.services: Service charges are typically included; extra tips are not required.

India.general: Tipping is appreciated but not always expected; cash tips alongside card payments are common.
India.restaurants: 10% is appreciated if no service charge is included; a small cash tip in addition to card payment is common.
India.taxis: Round up or add 10–15% for helpful drivers.
India.shops: No tipping in shops, but small tips to porters are appreciated.
India.services: 10–15% for hairdressers and personal care is customary.

Sri Lanka.general: Tipping is appreciated, especially in tourist areas; cash is preferred over card tips.
Sri Lanka.restaurants: 10% for good service is appreciated; service charge is sometimes included.
Sri Lanka.taxis: Negotiate fares in advance; tips are not expected.
Sri Lanka.services: 10% for spas and personal care is a kind gesture.

Nepal.general: Tipping is important for service workers, especially guides, porters, and hospitality staff.
Nepal.taxis: Negotiate fares beforehand; tips are not customary.
Nepal.services: 10–15% for trekking guides and porters is important and expected.

Pakistan.general: Tipping is appreciated in upscale settings; smaller venues don't generally expect it.
Pakistan.restaurants: 10% is appreciated in upscale restaurants; smaller venues don't expect tips.
Pakistan.taxis: Negotiated fares are standard; tips are not expected.
Pakistan.services: 10% for hairdressers is appreciated in urban areas.

Bangladesh.general: Tipping is welcomed in mid-range and upscale establishments.
Bangladesh.restaurants: 10% is welcomed in mid-range and upscale restaurants.
Bangladesh.taxis: Fares are negotiated; small tips are appreciated for good service.
Bangladesh.services: 10% for hairdressers and personal care is appreciated.

Hong Kong.general: A 10% service charge is usually included; an extra cash tip is a kind gesture.
Hong Kong.restaurants: 10% service charge is typically included; an extra 5–10% in cash is a warm gesture.
Hong Kong.taxis: Round up to the next dollar; large notes are not appreciated for small fares.

Taiwan.general: Tipping is not customary; excellent service is the standard expectation.
Taiwan.restaurants: Tipping is not customary; excellent service is the norm.
Taiwan.taxis: No tipping; use the metered fare.
Taiwan.services: Service charges are occasionally included at upscale venues; no tipping expected otherwise.

Australia.general: Tipping is not obligatory but 10% for good service is a welcome gesture.
Australia.restaurants: Not obligatory, but 10% for good service is appreciated.
Australia.taxis: Round up or add 10% for helpful service.
Australia.services: 10% for hairdressers and personal care is a nice gesture.

New Zealand.general: Tipping is not a strong tradition; 10% for good service is appreciated but not expected.
New Zealand.restaurants: Not customary, but 10% for good service is appreciated.
New Zealand.taxis: Round up; no strong tipping culture.
New Zealand.services: 10% for hairdressers is appreciated but not strongly expected.

Fiji.general: Tipping is appreciated in tourist areas; small tips for good service are welcomed.
Fiji.restaurants: 5–10% for good service is appreciated, especially in tourist areas.
Fiji.taxis: Round up or add a small tip for helpful drivers.
Fiji.services: 5–10% for spa and personal services is appreciated.

Sør-Afrika.general: Tipping is important — service workers often earn very low base wages and rely on gratuities.
Sør-Afrika.restaurants: 10–15% is expected and important; service staff often earn very low base wages.
Sør-Afrika.taxis: 10–15% for good service; for minibus taxis, round up.
Sør-Afrika.shops: No tipping in shops, but petrol station attendants expect a small tip.
Sør-Afrika.services: 10–15% for hairdressers and personal services is standard.

Egypt.general: Tipping (baksheesh) is deeply embedded in culture and expected across all service industries.
Egypt.restaurants: 10–15% (baksheesh) is expected and common across all service industries.
Egypt.taxis: Negotiate fares in advance; round up at the end.
Egypt.shops: Small tips for assistants and guides are appreciated.
Egypt.services: 10–15% for spas and personal services is customary.

Marokko.general: Tipping is customary and expected; service is rarely included in the bill.
Marokko.restaurants: 10% is customary; service is rarely included.
Marokko.taxis: Negotiate the fare first; round up at the end.
Marokko.shops: Small tips for assistance or guides are appreciated.
Marokko.services: 10% for hammam, spas, and personal care is standard.

Tunisia.general: Tipping is appreciated and common; service is not always included in the bill.
Tunisia.restaurants: 10% is appreciated; service may or may not be included.
Tunisia.taxis: Negotiate fares first; round up at the end.
Tunisia.services: 10% for personal care and hammam is appreciated.

Kenya.general: Tipping is expected, especially in tourist areas, and is important for service workers' livelihoods.
Kenya.restaurants: 10% is expected in tourist areas; tips support low-income service workers.
Kenya.taxis: Negotiate fares beforehand; tips are appreciated but not required.
Kenya.services: 10–15% for safari guides and lodges is important and expected.

Tanzania.general: Tipping is appreciated and important, especially for safari guides and trekking staff.
Tanzania.restaurants: 10% is appreciated; service charge is sometimes included.
Tanzania.taxis: Negotiate fares first; tips are appreciated.
Tanzania.services: 10–15% for safari guides and trekking porters is expected and important.

Ghana.general: Tipping is appreciated in restaurants and for personal services; it is not strongly expected.
Ghana.restaurants: 10% is appreciated in upscale and tourist restaurants.
Ghana.taxis: Negotiate fares first; tips are not obligatory.
Ghana.services: 10% for hairdressers and personal services is appreciated.

Nigeria.general: Tipping is common in upscale settings and for good service across most industries.
Nigeria.restaurants: 10% is appreciated in upscale restaurants; informal venues don't expect tips.
Nigeria.taxis: Negotiate fares before boarding; small tips are appreciated.
Nigeria.services: 10% for hairdressers and personal services is standard.

De forente arabiske emirater.general: Tipping is appreciated but not mandatory; service charges are often already included.
De forente arabiske emirater.restaurants: 10% service charge is often included; an extra 10% in cash is appreciated.
De forente arabiske emirater.taxis: No tipping expected; meters are used in Dubai and Abu Dhabi.
De forente arabiske emirater.services: 10–15% for spa, hotel, and personal services is appreciated.

Saudi-Arabia.general: Tipping is appreciated and welcomed, especially for hotel and restaurant service.
Saudi-Arabia.restaurants: 10% is appreciated; service charge is sometimes included.
Saudi-Arabia.taxis: No tipping expected; ride-hailing apps like Uber are common.
Saudi-Arabia.services: Small tips for porters and hotel staff are appreciated.

Qatar.general: Tipping is appreciated; service charges are common but extra tips are always welcomed.
Qatar.restaurants: 10% service charge is common; extra tips are welcomed by workers.
Qatar.taxis: No tipping expected; meters and ride-hailing apps are standard.
Qatar.services: 10% for hotel services and personal care is appreciated.

Kuwait.general: Tipping is a kind gesture and appreciated, though not strongly expected.
Kuwait.restaurants: 10% is appreciated; service charge may be included.
Kuwait.taxis: No tipping expected; negotiate fares for unmetered taxis.
Kuwait.services: 10% for hotel services and personal care is a kind gesture.

Oman.general: Tipping is welcomed and appreciated; Omanis are known for warm hospitality.
Oman.restaurants: 10% is appreciated; Omanis are known for gracious hospitality.
Oman.taxis: Negotiate fares beforehand; small tips are appreciated.
Oman.services: 10% for hotel staff and personal services is welcomed.

Bahrain.general: Tipping is appreciated; service charges are sometimes included in tourist restaurants.
Bahrain.restaurants: 10% is appreciated; service charge is sometimes included in tourist restaurants.
Bahrain.taxis: No tipping expected; ride-hailing apps are common.
Bahrain.services: 10% for hotel and personal services is appreciated.

Israel.general: Tipping is common practice; 10–12% is standard for restaurants and personal services.
Israel.restaurants: 10–12% is standard; service charge is sometimes added in tourist areas.
Israel.taxis: Round up to the nearest shekel; no strong tipping culture for taxis.
Israel.services: 10% for hairdressers and beauty services is customary.

Libanon.general: Tipping is common and appreciated; service is rarely included in the bill.
Libanon.restaurants: 10–15% is appreciated; service is rarely included in the bill.
Libanon.taxis: Negotiate fares beforehand; tips are not expected.
Libanon.services: 10–15% for hairdressers and personal services is standard.

Jordan.general: Tipping is appreciated and common for hotel, restaurant, and personal services.
Jordan.restaurants: 10% is appreciated; service charge is sometimes included.
Jordan.taxis: Negotiate fares before the journey; tips are not expected.
Jordan.services: 10% for hotel staff and personal services is appreciated.

about.title: About the app
about.description: Adjust the tip is a tip calculator for travellers. It provides culturally-aware tipping guidelines for 60+ countries across restaurants, taxis, shops, and services — so you always tip the right amount.
about.feedbackBody: Have a suggestion or found a bug? We'd love to hear from you.

help.title: How to use
help.intro: A quick guide to all the features in Adjust the tip.
help.step1Body: Choose the country you're in. The app can also detect your location automatically.
help.step2Body: Select restaurant, taxi, shop, or service. This sets the tip percentage to match local customs.
help.step3Body: Tap the book icon next to the country selector to read about local tipping culture for the selected country.
help.step4Body: Type the bill total. You can also scan a receipt with the camera button.
help.step5Body: Choose Unsatisfied, OK, or Excellent to set the tip percentage. Use Custom to enter your own.
help.step6Body: The result card shows the tip and total. Tap the round-up buttons for a round number, and tap Save to archive the calculation.
help.step7Body: Go to the Split tab to divide expenses among participants on a trip.
help.step8Body: Tap ★ next to a country in the picker to save it as a favourite. Favourites appear at the top of the list.

helpSplit.title: How Split works
helpSplit.intro: A guide to quick bill splitting.
helpSplit.step1Body: Select your country to set the currency automatically. Enter the total amount, or scan a receipt with the camera button.
helpSplit.step2Body: Choose the number of people, then select how to split: equally, by percentage, or custom amounts per person.
helpSplit.step3Body: Tap Save (requires Premium) to give the bill a name and add it to an existing trip, or create a new trip.

helpTrips.title: How Trips works
helpTrips.intro: A guide to tracking and settling shared expenses on a trip.
helpTrips.step1Body: Tap '+ New Trip', give it a name and add at least 2 participants. Your name from Settings is suggested automatically.
helpTrips.step2Body: Inside a trip, tap '+ Add Bill'. Enter a description, amount and who paid. Choose how to split: equally, by percentage, custom amounts, or scan a receipt.
helpTrips.step3Body: Tap 'Settle Up' to see the transfers needed to clear all debts. Amounts are converted automatically. Tap ○ next to a transfer to mark it as done.
helpTrips.step4Body: Tap the share button to send a group summary, or share directly with individual participants.

tip.tipsTab: Select a country to see the right tip percentage. Tap 'Split bill' to share costs.
tip.splitTab: Add participants and bills to split costs automatically.
tip.archiveTab: Saved calculations appear here. Tap one to add it to a trip.
